event: The Valdivia culture, one of the Americas' earliest settled societies, flourishes on Ecuador's Pacific coast and produces some of the continent's oldest ceramics.
The Machalilla and later Chorrera cultures develop along the coast, advancing pottery, agriculture, and long-distance trade networks.
Regional chiefdoms and trading cultures such as the Bahía, Jama-Coaque, and La Tolita emerge, the latter renowned for fine gold and platinum metalwork.
The Manteño-Huancavilca culture rises on the coast, building maritime trade routes that reach as far as Mesoamerica.
Highland peoples including the Cara, Puruhá, and Cañari form organized confederations and chiefdoms across the Andean valleys.
The Inca Empire under Túpac Yupanqui begins its conquest of the territory, subduing the Cañari after fierce resistance.
Inca emperor Huayna Capac consolidates control and develops Quito and Tomebamba (modern Cuenca) as major northern centers of the empire.
Huayna Capac dies, and the empire is divided between his sons Atahualpa, based in Quito, and Huáscar in Cuzco, sparking a civil war.
Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro captures Atahualpa at Cajamarca, beginning the Spanish conquest of the Inca realm.
Sebastián de Belalcázar founds the city of Quito on the ruins of the Inca city after Rumiñahui burns it rather than surrender it intact.
Bishop Tomás de Berlanga, blown off course en route to Peru, becomes the first European to reach the Galápagos Islands.
The Real Audiencia of Quito is established, creating a distinct colonial administrative district under the Viceroyalty of Peru.
A French geodesic expedition led by Charles Marie de La Condamine arrives to measure the equator, giving the future nation scientific renown.
Quito's elite proclaims an autonomous governing junta on August 10, an early cry for independence in Spanish America.
Antonio José de Sucre defeats royalist forces at the Battle of Pichincha on May 24, securing independence and incorporating Quito into Gran Colombia.
Ecuador secedes from Gran Colombia and becomes an independent republic, with Juan José Flores as its first president.
Ecuador formally annexes the Galápagos Islands, asserting sovereignty over the Pacific archipelago.
Naturalist Charles Darwin visits the Galápagos Islands aboard HMS Beagle, gathering observations central to his theory of evolution.
A national crisis of competing regional governments nearly fractures the country before Gabriel García Moreno seizes power.
Gabriel García Moreno begins a theocratic, conservative rule that modernizes infrastructure while tightly binding the state to the Catholic Church.
García Moreno is assassinated in Quito, ending his dominance of mid-century Ecuadorian politics.
Eloy Alfaro leads the Liberal Revolution, ushering in secular reforms, separation of church and state, and modernization.
The Guayaquil-Quito railway is completed, connecting the coast and highlands and transforming the national economy.
Former president Eloy Alfaro is killed by a mob in Quito, a violent end to the liberal era's leading figure.
The July Revolution (Revolución Juliana) overthrows the plutocratic order, leading to financial and social reforms and the founding of the central bank.
War with Peru breaks out over disputed Amazonian territory, ending in a costly Ecuadorian defeat.
The Rio de Janeiro Protocol forces Ecuador to cede vast Amazonian lands to Peru, a humiliation that fuels nationalist grievances for decades.
The 'Glorious Revolution' (La Gloriosa) returns populist José María Velasco Ibarra to the presidency amid mass uprisings.
Ecuador becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
A period of relative democratic stability begins as Galo Plaza Lasso takes office and completes his term peacefully.
A military junta seizes power, beginning a renewed cycle of military intervention in national politics.
Large oil reserves are discovered in the Amazon region, soon reshaping the national economy.
A military government takes power as Amazonian oil begins flowing, launching an oil-funded development boom.
Ecuador returns to civilian rule as Jaime Roldós Aguilera is elected president under a new constitution.
President Jaime Roldós dies in a plane crash, and a brief border clash with Peru flares at Paquisha.
The Cenepa War erupts with Peru over the still-disputed Amazon border, ending in a ceasefire after weeks of fighting.
Ecuador and Peru sign the Brasília Presidential Act, definitively settling their long-running border dispute.
Amid a severe financial crisis, Ecuador abandons the sucre and adopts the U.S. dollar as its official currency.
President Jamil Mahuad is ousted in a coup backed by indigenous protesters and junior military officers.
President Lucio Gutiérrez is removed from office by Congress amid mass street protests in Quito.
Rafael Correa becomes president, launching his leftist 'Citizens' Revolution' and a new political era.
Voters approve a new constitution that, among other firsts, grants legally enforceable rights to nature.
A police revolt over benefits escalates into what the government calls a coup attempt against President Correa.
A magnitude 7.8 earthquake strikes the northern coast, killing hundreds and devastating cities like Pedernales and Portoviejo.
Lenín Moreno is elected president and gradually breaks with his predecessor Correa, reorienting national policy.
Mass protests erupt over the removal of fuel subsidies, forcing the government to temporarily relocate from Quito and reverse the measure.
President Guillermo Lasso invokes 'muerte cruzada' to dissolve the legislature and call early elections amid impeachment proceedings.
Presidential candidate Fernando Villavicencio is assassinated, underscoring a surge of cartel-driven violence gripping the country.
President Daniel Noboa declares an 'internal armed conflict' against drug gangs after armed men storm a TV station live on air.
